questions.explanation: Lesson 07: filter/withColumn are narrow — each partition is independent. groupBy/join/distinct are wide — they need rows with the same key to land on the same executor, which means writing data to disk and re-reading it across the network.

questions.question: When should you reach for `approx_count_distinct()` instead of `countDistinct()`?
options: When you don't need the exact unique count and the column has many distinct values, a much cheaper estimate (typically ±2%) is acceptable
They're identical in cost; the choice is purely about the API name your team prefers
countDistinct works on any column type; approx_count_distinct only supports numeric columns
approx_count_distinct caches results across queries, so it's only fast on the second call onward
questions.correctAnswer: When you don't need the exact unique count and the column has many distinct values, a much cheaper estimate (typically ±2%) is acceptable
questions.explanation: Lesson 08: countDistinct shuffles all values to one place to count exactly — expensive for high-cardinality columns. approx_count_distinct uses HyperLogLog and is dramatically cheaper at the cost of a small error margin. The choice is cost vs precision.

questions.question: Daily regional summary, monthly billing, and quarterly regulatory pivot — what do these three NordGrid pipelines have in common?
options: Same toolkit — filter to pre-trim rows, groupBy to define groups, agg with aliases for metrics
Same shuffle partition count, hand-tuned to 1024 across every Gold pipeline for consistency
Same UDF that handles regional totals, called from each pipeline's final stage
Same source table — each pipeline materializes a private clone of Silver before aggregating
questions.correctAnswer: Same toolkit — filter to pre-trim rows, groupBy to define groups, agg with aliases for metrics
questions.explanation: Lesson 09: aggregation feels varied (single key, multi-key, pivoted) but the building blocks are the same. The patterns differ in how you combine the same primitives, not in the primitives themselves.

questions.question: What's the difference between `count('*')` and `count('daily_kw')` in an aggregation?
options: count('*') counts every row including those with nulls; count('daily_kw') counts only rows where that column is NOT null
Both count rows identically; the difference is performance — count('col') uses column statistics
count('col') counts unique values in the column; count('*') counts the total row count
count('*') raises if any column has nulls; count('col') is the safe form for null-tolerant rows
questions.correctAnswer: count('*') counts every row including those with nulls; count('daily_kw') counts only rows where that column is NOT null
questions.explanation: Lesson 10 mistake: aggregation functions handle nulls per-column. count('*') is row-count; count(column) is non-null-count. sum/avg also ignore nulls. Knowing this avoids 'why is my count 1500 lower than my row count?' confusion.

questions.question: Why does NordGrid keep meter readings, meter metadata, and substation references in separate tables instead of one big table?
options: Different update cadences — readings change daily, metadata rarely; separation keeps storage compact and updates simple
Spark imposes a per-table size limit, so very large datasets must be split across several physically separate tables
Tables in different formats can be queried in parallel — mixing CSV and Parquet in one table is forbidden by Spark
Joining is essentially free, so any layout works — separate tables are an arbitrary historical convention by now
questions.correctAnswer: Different update cadences — readings change daily, metadata rarely; separation keeps storage compact and updates simple
questions.explanation: Lesson 01: separation reflects real change frequencies. Denormalizing a 500K-row metadata table into every reading row would multiply storage and force every metadata change to rewrite the entire reading history. Joins are how you reassemble at query time.

questions.question: An inner join between 500,000 readings and 498,200 meters returns 498,200 rows. What is the danger in this result?
options: Inner joins silently drop unmatched rows from BOTH sides — orphan readings disappear without warning, hiding data quality issues
Inner joins always duplicate rows when the right side has multiple matches per key
Inner joins are guaranteed deterministic, so any drop is repeatable and therefore safe to ignore
Inner joins corrupt the schema by aliasing all key columns; the result needs manual rename before use
questions.correctAnswer: Inner joins silently drop unmatched rows from BOTH sides — orphan readings disappear without warning, hiding data quality issues
questions.explanation: Lesson 02 warning: inner joins are silent filters. If your readings/metadata join shrinks the row count, those rows are gone — and the issue (a meter that emits readings without a metadata entry) gets buried. Use a left join + null check when you want auditability.

questions.question: Which join type guarantees that the result has the same number of rows as the LEFT input?
options: Left join — every left row is preserved; right-side columns are null when there's no match
Left join always equals MAX(left, right) rows, which gives you both sides combined
Left join's row count depends on right-side matches per key — usually larger than left input
Left join's row count is unspecified; Spark may drop rows for performance even when there are matches
questions.correctAnswer: Left join — every left row is preserved; right-side columns are null when there's no match
questions.explanation: Lesson 03: a left join never drops a left row. Use it for enrichment when the left table is your source of truth — the row count equals input regardless of metadata coverage. Pair with `assert df_out.count() == df_in.count()` for safety.

questions.question: When is a full outer join the right tool?
options: Reconciliation — surface left-only, right-only, AND matched rows; the nulls in the result identify the gaps
Enrichment — add right-side columns to every left row without changing the input row count at all
Filtering — keep only rows that appear in both inputs, like an inner join with renamed key columns
Deduplication — collapse duplicates that appear in either input down to one canonical winning row
questions.correctAnswer: Reconciliation — surface left-only, right-only, AND matched rows; the nulls in the result identify the gaps
questions.explanation: Lesson 04: full outer keeps everything. NordGrid's pattern: join the meter inventory to the readings — left-only rows = inventory entries that never reported, right-only rows = readings from unknown meters. The nulls tell the story.

questions.question: What's the most dangerous thing about cross joins in production?
options: An accidental cross (omitted condition or trivially-true condition) silently multiplies rows — 500K × 500K = 250 billion, crashing the cluster
Cross joins always raise an explicit error in Spark; the danger is purely educational and never appears in real pipelines
Cross joins lose ordering guarantees, so any subsequent window functions become non-deterministic across runs
Cross joins shuffle data twice in succession, doubling the typical join cost without changing the final correctness
questions.correctAnswer: An accidental cross (omitted condition or trivially-true condition) silently multiplies rows — 500K × 500K = 250 billion, crashing the cluster
questions.explanation: Lesson 05: cross joins are output multipliers — `left × right`. Spark won't stop you from joining on `lit(True)`. The signature symptom: a result far larger than either input. Always sanity-check row counts after a join.

questions.question: How are semi joins and anti joins different from inner/left/full joins?
options: They are FILTERS — they use the right DataFrame only to decide which left-side rows to keep (semi) or drop (anti), and add NO columns from the right
They are outer-join shortcuts — the same as left/right joins but with a smaller wire format
They are performance variants of inner join, optimized by skipping the row-combination step
They exist for ANSI-SQL compatibility only, so PySpark code translates cleanly to other engines
questions.correctAnswer: They are FILTERS — they use the right DataFrame only to decide which left-side rows to keep (semi) or drop (anti), and add NO columns from the right
questions.explanation: Lesson 06: semi answers 'which left rows have a match?' (keeps them); anti answers 'which left rows have NO match?' (keeps those). Output columns come only from the left side — they're filters disguised as joins.

questions.question: When does column ambiguity (`Reference 'region' is ambiguous`) typically occur after a join?
options: Joining on a Column expression like `df_a['region'] == df_b['region']` — both region columns survive; using `on='region'` (string) collapses them
Whenever the two DataFrames share any column name, regardless of how the join condition is written in code
Only on left joins; inner and full outer joins always rename duplicate columns automatically with a numeric suffix
When the DataFrames have different schemas — Spark refuses to merge non-identical schemas without explicit aliasing
questions.correctAnswer: Joining on a Column expression like `df_a['region'] == df_b['region']` — both region columns survive; using `on='region'` (string) collapses them
questions.explanation: Lesson 07: `on='region'` keeps a single region column; `df_a.region == df_b.region` keeps both. Fix by joining on string keys when possible, aliasing the DataFrames, or dropping the duplicate column right after the join.

questions.question: What is a non-equi join?
options: A join whose condition uses a comparison other than `=` — `BETWEEN`, `<`, `>` — typically for range lookups against tier brackets
A join that intentionally returns no rows, used to validate that two datasets share no overlapping keys at all
A join on multiple equality conditions chained with AND, also called a multi-key or compound-key join
A self-join in which a DataFrame is joined to itself via a non-injective key for hierarchical lookups
questions.correctAnswer: A join whose condition uses a comparison other than `=` — `BETWEEN`, `<`, `>` — typically for range lookups against tier brackets
questions.explanation: Lesson 08: equi-joins (`=`) cover 90% of cases. Non-equi joins handle ranges — e.g. matching `daily_kw` to a tier table where each tier defines `min_kw <= reading < max_kw`. They are more expensive to plan because hash-based join strategies don't apply.

questions.question: What is NordGrid's defensive habit after every join in a multi-step join pipeline?
options: Assert the row count — a left join shouldn't change the left side's count, so any drift signals a bad condition or duplicate keys
Cache the join result so the next stage doesn't recompute it from the source files on disk
Re-cast every column to its expected type, since join can promote int to long during merge
Write the result to a checkpoint Parquet so the pipeline can recover from a downstream failure
questions.correctAnswer: Assert the row count — a left join shouldn't change the left side's count, so any drift signals a bad condition or duplicate keys
questions.explanation: Lesson 09: row-count assertions are the cheapest debugging tool you have. A silent fan-out from duplicate keys, or silent loss from a bad inner join, becomes an explicit failure where it happens, not 4 transformations later.

questions.question: Why is a broadcast join much cheaper than a shuffle hash / sort-merge join when one side is small?
options: Broadcast sends the SMALL DataFrame to every executor in full; the LARGE DataFrame is matched in place, with no shuffle of the large side at all
Broadcast joins use a faster join algorithm (sort-merge) that shuffle joins can't access
Broadcast joins always run on a single executor, eliminating cross-node coordination overhead
Broadcast joins skip the join condition for matching rows, applying it only to the residual unmatched rows
questions.correctAnswer: Broadcast sends the SMALL DataFrame to every executor in full; the LARGE DataFrame is matched in place, with no shuffle of the large side at all
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: shuffling a multi-billion-row table is the expensive part. If the right side fits in executor memory (default threshold ~10 MB), Spark broadcasts it and does the join locally on each partition of the large table — no large-side shuffle.

questions.question: Does `spark.read.parquet('path/')` immediately load data into memory?
options: No — it returns a DataFrame referencing the files; the actual read happens when an action triggers, with column pruning and predicate pushdown
Yes — it loads the entire file into the driver's memory at the time the spark.read.parquet call is made
Partial — it loads the schema and the first row group eagerly, then defers the rest until an action triggers
Yes for CSV and JSON; Parquet is the only format Spark loads lazily, since it has self-describing metadata
questions.correctAnswer: No — it returns a DataFrame referencing the files; the actual read happens when an action triggers, with column pruning and predicate pushdown
questions.explanation: Lesson 01: `spark.read` is lazy like every other DataFrame operation. It records intent. The reader picks up filters and column lists from later transformations and reads only what's needed when you finally trigger execution.

questions.question: Why does `inferSchema=True` on CSV cost so much?
options: Spark must scan the entire file TWICE — once to guess column types, then again to actually read with those types
It allocates ~3× the file size in driver heap to hold the inference state, which can OOM on large files
It rewrites the file on disk in column order to enable later predicate pushdown
It triggers a schema refresh on every read, even when an explicit StructType has already been supplied
questions.correctAnswer: Spark must scan the entire file TWICE — once to guess column types, then again to actually read with those types
questions.explanation: Lesson 02 warning: CSV has no type metadata, so `inferSchema=True` does a full pass to guess types and a second to load. On a 500 GB archive that doubles read cost. In production, supply a `StructType` schema explicitly.

questions.question: What does `spark.read.json('path/')` expect by default?
options: JSON Lines (newline-delimited JSON) — one complete JSON object per line; the standard format for log/event data
A single JSON object spanning the whole file with a top-level array of records
A YAML-compatible variant; Spark accepts both YAML and JSON via the same reader
A Spark-specific binary serialization of JSON optimized for distributed reads
questions.correctAnswer: JSON Lines (newline-delimited JSON) — one complete JSON object per line; the standard format for log/event data
questions.explanation: Lesson 03: JSONL is the default for streaming/event ingestion. For multi-line JSON documents (a single object spanning many lines), set `option('multiLine', True)`. Nested objects come back as StructType columns.

questions.question: Why does Parquet rarely need an explicit schema when reading?
options: Parquet files embed the schema (column names, types, nullability) in the file metadata — Spark reads it directly with no inference required
Parquet is schemaless; Spark applies a default StructType with all-string columns at read time
Parquet stores the schema in a sidecar `.schema.json` next to each data file, which Spark loads automatically
Parquet uses the source CSV's schema by default; Spark looks up the original ingestion schema from the catalog
questions.correctAnswer: Parquet files embed the schema (column names, types, nullability) in the file metadata — Spark reads it directly with no inference required
questions.explanation: Lesson 04: Parquet is self-describing. The schema travels with the data, so the reader gets correct types — including dates and nullability — for free. This is one reason Parquet is the standard for processed analytical data.

questions.question: Why is schema inference dangerous in long-lived production pipelines?
options: Inference depends on the sample's content — if the source produces values that change the inferred type, downstream code that assumed the old type breaks silently
It requires writing temporary inference files to local disk, which can fill up the driver's filesystem on large inputs
Inference is non-deterministic across Spark versions, so an upgrade can quietly change the inferred column types
It only works the first time a pipeline runs; subsequent runs cache and reuse the original (possibly wrong) types
questions.correctAnswer: Inference depends on the sample's content — if the source produces values that change the inferred type, downstream code that assumed the old type breaks silently
questions.explanation: Lesson 05: a real NordGrid incident. inferSchema is non-deterministic across data drift. An explicit StructType pins the contract: if the data actually changes, you get an explicit error instead of silent breakage at the analytics layer.

questions.question: What's the biggest risk of using `mode('append')` for an idempotent daily pipeline?
options: If the pipeline runs twice for the same day (retry after a transient failure), the second run appends duplicates — output silently doubles
Append silently fails when the target directory has fewer files than the source, so partial writes are common
Append requires manual coordination with the cluster manager; missing that step deletes the existing data on disk
Append rewrites the entire output every run, so it is actually equivalent to overwrite for idempotent jobs
questions.correctAnswer: If the pipeline runs twice for the same day (retry after a transient failure), the second run appends duplicates — output silently doubles
questions.explanation: Lesson 06: append doesn't dedupe. For idempotency, either (a) use `overwrite` partitioned by date so a re-run replaces just that day, or (b) write to a staging path and use a transactional copy. Append + retries = duplicates.

questions.question: How do you control how many output files a `df.write.parquet(...)` produces?
options: Determined by partition count at write time — use `coalesce(n)` to reduce or `repartition(n)` to redistribute first
Set `option('maxFiles', n)` on the writer; Spark splits the output until exactly n output files are produced
Spark always writes one file per executor regardless of partition count; scale the executors to control the count
The cluster manager decides the count based on available resources at write time; it can't be controlled in code
questions.correctAnswer: Determined by partition count at write time — use `coalesce(n)` to reduce or `repartition(n)` to redistribute first
questions.explanation: Lesson 07: one output file per partition. coalesce(n) reduces partitions without a shuffle (good when going down). repartition(n) reshuffles — necessary when going up or when partition sizes are unbalanced.

questions.question: Why is `partitionBy('meter_id')` a bad idea on a 500,000-meter table?
options: It creates 500K directories — the small-files problem; metadata operations dominate read time, often more than the data scan
partitionBy doesn't accept high-cardinality columns; Spark throws an analysis exception at plan time before write
It triggers a full sort of the input, which costs more than the join the partitioning was meant to optimize for
It doubles storage cost because each row gets duplicated into both partition and global indexes on disk in parallel
questions.correctAnswer: It creates 500K directories — the small-files problem; metadata operations dominate read time, often more than the data scan
questions.explanation: Lesson 08 warning: partition columns should have LOW cardinality. Region (4 values) is great. Date (~1K values) is fine if you query by date often. Meter_id (500K) blows up directory listing time and turns a 5-second scan into 5 minutes of metadata operations.

questions.question: What's the fundamental layout difference between CSV and Parquet?
options: CSV is row-oriented (each row's columns sit together); Parquet is column-oriented — enabling column pruning and per-column compression
CSV stores values as Unicode strings; Parquet stores them as ASCII bytes for compactness and faster scanning
CSV files are seekable; Parquet files require streaming through the entire file from start to end on every read
They differ only in the field separator — CSV uses commas, Parquet uses null bytes — and are otherwise identical
questions.correctAnswer: CSV is row-oriented (each row's columns sit together); Parquet is column-oriented — enabling column pruning and per-column compression
questions.explanation: Lesson 09: row-oriented favors transactional writes (append a row, you write all columns together). Column-oriented favors analytical reads (read just the columns you need, compress each column with the codec best suited to its values). This is why Parquet dominates analytics.

questions.question: In NordGrid's pipeline, why does CSV stay at the Bronze layer while Parquet is used for Silver and Gold?
options: Source systems produce CSV so Bronze accepts it as-is; Silver/Gold use Parquet for the schema-bearing, column-pruned, compressed analytical layout
Parquet is a NordGrid-licensed format authorized only for processed analytical data, not raw ingestion from sources
CSV files compress better than Parquet on raw ingestion data, but Parquet wins for aggregated outputs at the Gold layer
Spark requires this exact arrangement; mixing formats across pipeline layers raises a planner error at submission time
questions.correctAnswer: Source systems produce CSV so Bronze accepts it as-is; Silver/Gold use Parquet for the schema-bearing, column-pruned, compressed analytical layout
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: format follows the pipeline stage. Bronze prioritizes faithful capture of whatever the source produced (CSV/JSON). Silver/Gold prioritize fast analytical reads, where Parquet's column layout, compression, and self-describing schema pay off.

questions.question: Why are functions from `pyspark.sql.functions` (like `upper`, `year`, `round`) so much faster than wrapping the same logic in a Python UDF?
options: Spark compiles them to JVM bytecode and runs them on the executors; Python is involved only when building the plan, not when rows are processed
They run on dedicated GPU kernels available in Spark since version 3.0, which UDFs cannot access
They cache results on the executor's local disk between invocations of the same expression on a partition
They use a static-analysis fast path that skips Catalyst entirely, avoiding the optimizer's per-row overhead
questions.correctAnswer: Spark compiles them to JVM bytecode and runs them on the executors; Python is involved only when building the plan, not when rows are processed
questions.explanation: Lesson 01: built-ins are JVM-native. Each row stays in the JVM through the whole pipeline. UDFs serialize each row to Python and back per row — much slower and Catalyst can't reason about the operation.

questions.question: When source data packs multiple values into a delimited string (e.g. `'NORTH-METER-001'`), what's the cleanest way to extract the parts?
options: Use `split('-')` to produce an array, then `element_at(arr, n)` to pull each piece — readable and avoids regex
Wrap a Python `str.split` call in a UDF; it's the only way to get clean per-row indexing
Use `regexp_extract` with a complex backreference pattern, since regex is the only reliable way to handle delimited fields
Cast the column to a struct type; Spark splits on the delimiter automatically based on the struct schema
questions.correctAnswer: Use `split('-')` to produce an array, then `element_at(arr, n)` to pull each piece — readable and avoids regex
questions.explanation: Lesson 02: split + element_at handles most delimited-string parsing without writing regex. Reserve regexp_replace/regexp_extract for genuinely irregular patterns.

questions.question: When dates arrive as strings in a non-ISO format (e.g. `'01/March/2026'`), what's the right way to convert them to DateType?
options: Use `to_date(col, 'dd/MMMM/yyyy')` — `cast('date')` only handles the ISO format YYYY-MM-DD
Use `cast('date')` with the source format auto-detected; Spark tries every common format until one fits the data
Use `regexp_extract` to pull year, month, day separately; then concat into ISO format and cast at the end
Wrap Python's `datetime.strptime` in a UDF — the only option, since PySpark has no format-aware date parser
questions.correctAnswer: Use `to_date(col, 'dd/MMMM/yyyy')` — `cast('date')` only handles the ISO format YYYY-MM-DD
questions.explanation: Lesson 03: cast assumes ISO-8601 dates. For any other format, `to_date()` (or `to_timestamp()`) takes an explicit format string. Parse once at the Bronze→Silver boundary; then DateType all the way through.

questions.question: What's the difference between `greatest(col('a'), col('b'), col('c'))` and `max(col('a'))`?
options: `greatest()` compares values ACROSS COLUMNS in the SAME row; `max()` aggregates DOWN ROWS for one column
They're equivalent — both return the largest value across whatever input you pass them
`greatest()` works only on numbers; `max()` works on strings, dates, and numerics
`max()` is faster because it uses statistics; `greatest()` scans every row even when statistics exist
questions.correctAnswer: `greatest()` compares values ACROSS COLUMNS in the SAME row; `max()` aggregates DOWN ROWS for one column
questions.explanation: Lesson 04: greatest/least are row-level — same row, multiple columns. max/min are aggregate functions — same column, multiple rows. They look similar but live on different axes; mixing them up is a classic confusion.

questions.question: What happens to the row count when you `explode()` an array column?
options: Rows MULTIPLY — one row with an N-element array becomes N rows; other columns are duplicated; the row count fans out
Row count stays the same; explode replaces the array column with a struct column that holds the elements positionally
Row count drops to one summary row, with the array values moved into a new column as a comma-separated string
Spark refuses to explode arrays larger than 100 elements without an explicit `option('limit', n)` on the call site
questions.correctAnswer: Rows MULTIPLY — one row with an N-element array becomes N rows; other columns are duplicated; the row count fans out
questions.explanation: Lesson 05 warning: explode is row-multiplying. 500,000 meters × 24 hourly samples = 12 million rows after exploding. Plan for the size; pre-filter the array if you don't need every element.

questions.question: What does `coalesce(col('primary'), col('backup'), lit(0.0))` return?
options: The FIRST non-null value among the arguments — primary if non-null, otherwise backup, otherwise the literal 0.0
Always the last argument, treating earlier ones as default values applied only when nulls are encountered
All three arguments concatenated as strings, separated by commas, with nulls rendered as the empty string
Null whenever ANY argument is null — it is a strict any-null fallthrough that propagates missing values forward
questions.correctAnswer: The FIRST non-null value among the arguments — primary if non-null, otherwise backup, otherwise the literal 0.0
questions.explanation: Lesson 06: coalesce builds a fallback chain across COLUMNS. Useful when readings come from multiple sources (primary feed, backup cell, manual entry). Distinct from `fillna({col: 0})`, which only fills nulls in one specific column with one literal.

questions.question: What's NordGrid's convention about WHERE in the pipeline null handling should happen?
options: At the Bronze→Silver boundary — by Silver, every column has well-defined non-null semantics, so downstream code never special-cases nulls
At the Gold layer — aggregations naturally absorb nulls, so handling them is cheapest after the heavy aggregation has run
At the dashboard layer — BI tools handle nulls more flexibly than Spark's null-handling functions, so let them do it
Spread across every layer — null handling is too contextual to centralize at a single boundary in the pipeline
questions.correctAnswer: At the Bronze→Silver boundary — by Silver, every column has well-defined non-null semantics, so downstream code never special-cases nulls
questions.explanation: Lesson 07: pushing null handling into one boundary eliminates an entire class of recurring bugs. The Silver contract becomes: 'columns are clean, nulls are explicit and intentional, everyone downstream can rely on it.'

questions.question: How does a window function like `lag(col('daily_kw'), 1)` differ from `groupBy('meter_id').agg(lag(...))`?
options: Window functions ADD a column without reducing the row count; groupBy().agg() COLLAPSES rows into one row per group
Window functions are syntactic sugar for groupBy().agg() — they produce identical output and identical execution plans
Window functions only work on numeric columns; groupBy works on any type including strings, dates, and structs
Window functions are SQL-only; the DataFrame API uses groupBy for the same operations under the hood
questions.correctAnswer: Window functions ADD a column without reducing the row count; groupBy().agg() COLLAPSES rows into one row per group
questions.explanation: Lesson 08: window functions preserve every input row and decorate it with values computed across a window of related rows. groupBy reduces; window decorates. Day-over-day deltas, running totals, and rank-within-group all need windows, not groupBy.

questions.question: Why are Python UDFs typically 10–100× slower than equivalent built-in expressions?
options: Each row is serialized to Python and back, paying that cost twice per row; Catalyst can't reason about UDF logic so pushdown is skipped
Spark deliberately throttles UDFs to discourage their use in favour of the built-in functions and expressions
UDFs run on a single executor instead of being distributed across the cluster, which limits their parallelism
UDFs can't access the JVM's columnar memory layout, so they fall back to Python lists per row at execution time
questions.correctAnswer: Each row is serialized to Python and back, paying that cost twice per row; Catalyst can't reason about UDF logic so pushdown is skipped
questions.explanation: Lesson 09: UDFs pay a per-row serialization cost twice and become opaque to the optimizer. Always check pyspark.sql.functions before writing one. Use `when().otherwise()` chains for conditional logic — they're built-ins, fast, and Catalyst-aware.

questions.question: Order these from FASTEST to SLOWEST: built-in Column functions, expr() SQL strings, Python UDFs.
options: Built-in functions ≈ expr() (same Catalyst plan) > Python UDF (much slower due to JVM↔Python serialization)
UDF > expr() > built-in — UDFs win because Python compiles to native code via JIT compilation at runtime
expr() > UDF > built-in — SQL parsing skips most of Catalyst's optimization overhead per query call site
All three perform identically in practice; the choice is purely about which is easier for the team to read
questions.correctAnswer: Built-in functions ≈ expr() (same Catalyst plan) > Python UDF (much slower due to JVM↔Python serialization)
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: the performance hierarchy is built-in ≈ expr() ≫ UDF. expr() parses to the same Catalyst plan as the Column API. UDFs sit far below because of cross-language overhead. Decision order: built-in first, expr() if SQL is clearer, UDF only if no other option.

questions.question: Why must you call `df.createOrReplaceTempView('readings')` before writing `spark.sql('SELECT * FROM readings')`?
options: SQL references tables by NAME — a Python variable doesn't expose a name to the SQL parser; the view binds the DataFrame to a name in the catalog
Spark SQL only works in cluster mode and createOrReplaceTempView is the cluster-mode signaller for the executor pool
Spark SQL needs the data persisted; createOrReplaceTempView writes the DataFrame to local disk before the SQL query runs
It's required by Hive metastore configuration; without it, SQL falls back to a slower local parser without optimizations
questions.correctAnswer: SQL references tables by NAME — a Python variable doesn't expose a name to the SQL parser; the view binds the DataFrame to a name in the catalog
questions.explanation: Lesson 01: `createOrReplaceTempView` is the bridge — it gives the DataFrame a name SQL can bind to. The DataFrame itself isn't materialized; the view is just a logical alias.

questions.question: What does `spark.sql('SELECT region, sum(daily_kw) FROM readings GROUP BY region')` return?
options: A standard PySpark DataFrame — chainable with DataFrame ops, registerable as another view, writable to Parquet
A list of Python tuples representing the result rows, materialized eagerly on the driver
A Pandas DataFrame, materialized in driver memory at query execution time
A SQL string ready to be passed into a downstream query for further parsing
questions.correctAnswer: A standard PySpark DataFrame — chainable with DataFrame ops, registerable as another view, writable to Parquet
questions.explanation: Lesson 02: spark.sql returns a DataFrame just like spark.read does. SQL and the DataFrame API freely interoperate — you can write a SQL aggregation and then chain `.filter()` on the result.

questions.question: If SQL and the DataFrame API produce the same execution plan, why support both?
options: Each is clearer in different scenarios — SQL for declarative analytics queries, DataFrame API for procedural ETL; Catalyst compiles both to the same plan
DataFrame API is the modern path; SQL support exists only for backwards compatibility with legacy SQL servers and migrations
DataFrame API is always faster than SQL because the SQL parser is bypassed entirely on every query execution path
SQL is required for cluster mode; the DataFrame API runs only in local mode for development and small-scale workloads
questions.correctAnswer: Each is clearer in different scenarios — SQL for declarative analytics queries, DataFrame API for procedural ETL; Catalyst compiles both to the same plan
questions.explanation: Lesson 03: same engine, two front doors. Choose by READABILITY for the task — analytics teams reading SQL fluently; engineers wiring up testable, IDE-aware ETL using the DataFrame API.

questions.question: What's the practical difference between a temporary view and a global temporary view?
options: Temp view is session-scoped (visible only inside the creating session); global temp view lives in `global_temp` and is visible to ALL sessions in the application
Global temp views are persisted to disk so they survive application restarts, while temp views vanish on session close
Temp views are faster; global temp views add a metadata lookup overhead per query call across the cluster nodes
They behave identically; the two methods exist only for historical-API compatibility reasons since Spark 2.0
questions.correctAnswer: Temp view is session-scoped (visible only inside the creating session); global temp view lives in `global_temp` and is visible to ALL sessions in the application
questions.explanation: Lesson 04: scope is the only meaningful difference. Default to temp views — the smaller scope avoids cross-pipeline collisions. Reach for global temp views only when you genuinely need cross-session sharing.

questions.question: What does NordGrid's hybrid pipeline pattern look like?
options: DataFrame API for procedural Bronze→Silver (load, cast, filter), then register Silver and use SQL for declarative Gold queries (joins, groupBy)
SQL for the entire pipeline; the DataFrame API is reserved only for non-tabular data like images and binary blobs
DataFrame API for ingestion, SQL for transformation, and the DataFrame API again for output writing to the target storage
Always start with SQL for readability and convert to DataFrame at the very last step before writing the final result
questions.correctAnswer: DataFrame API for procedural Bronze→Silver (load, cast, filter), then register Silver and use SQL for declarative Gold queries (joins, groupBy)
questions.explanation: Lesson 05: each stage gets the tool that's clearest there. Procedural ETL — DataFrame API. Declarative analytics — SQL. Mixing freely is the productive pattern; forcing one or the other costs readability.

questions.question: What's the SQL equivalent of method chaining `df.filter(...).groupBy(...).agg(...)`?
options: Common Table Expressions — `WITH step1 AS (...), step2 AS (...) SELECT ... FROM step2`; one named transformation per CTE
Stored procedures — encapsulated SQL that exposes a callable interface to the rest of the pipeline runtime
Triggers — declarative rules that fire on every write to the underlying tables, modifying rows in flight
Anonymous subqueries — Spark inlines them into the parent query at planning time, with no name binding step
questions.correctAnswer: Common Table Expressions — `WITH step1 AS (...), step2 AS (...) SELECT ... FROM step2`; one named transformation per CTE
questions.explanation: Lesson 06: CTEs let SQL flow as a step-by-step pipeline instead of nested subqueries. Each step gets a name; later steps reference earlier ones. The same intent as DataFrame chaining, expressed in SQL.

questions.question: How does `UPPER(region)` in Spark SQL relate to `upper(col('region'))` in the DataFrame API?
options: Same operation — Spark SQL functions and DataFrame API functions compile to the same internal expression; only syntax differs
SQL UPPER is significantly slower because the SQL parser adds latency on every individual function call site
DataFrame upper handles Unicode correctly; SQL UPPER is ASCII-only by default and ignores non-ASCII characters
They differ in null handling — SQL UPPER returns empty string on null, while DataFrame upper preserves the null
questions.correctAnswer: Same operation — Spark SQL functions and DataFrame API functions compile to the same internal expression; only syntax differs
questions.explanation: Lesson 07: every function category — string, date, numeric, conditional, aggregation — has parallel API and SQL forms backed by the same JVM implementation. Same engine, different syntax.

questions.question: Why is `spark.catalog.tableExists('my_view')` useful in a SQL-heavy pipeline?
options: Defensive programming — verify required views are registered before queries, so a missing view fails fast with a clear message
Spark requires the existence check before every SELECT in production for security audit logging compliance
It is a performance optimization — the check caches the table's plan for reuse on later queries within the session
It deletes the view if it exists, returning the freed catalog slot to the application for reuse by other pipelines
questions.correctAnswer: Defensive programming — verify required views are registered before queries, so a missing view fails fast with a clear message
questions.explanation: Lesson 08: catalog checks are cheap and turn 'view missing in step 7 of 12' into 'view missing — pre-flight check failed.' Same pattern as schema validation: spend a few lines to fail clearly at the boundary.

questions.question: Which factor matters MOST when choosing between SQL and the DataFrame API at NordGrid?
options: Team consistency — the codebase should apply one interface consistently for similar operations so reviewers don't relearn conventions per file
Personal preference of the engineer writing the code at any given moment, with style chosen file-by-file as desired
Always SQL because analysts read it more easily; the DataFrame API is for engineers writing ETL exclusively
Always the DataFrame API because it is faster and SQL adds parsing overhead on every query execution call
questions.correctAnswer: Team consistency — the codebase should apply one interface consistently for similar operations so reviewers don't relearn conventions per file
questions.explanation: Lesson 09: convention beats preference. A consistent codebase is easier to review, easier to debug at 3 AM, and easier to onboard into. Pick a rule (e.g. ETL = API, analytics = SQL) and stick to it.

questions.question: After completing the junior PySpark track, what foundation has been built?
options: Every operation a junior data engineer uses daily — SparkSession, DataFrames, select/filter, transformations, aggregations, joins, I/O, functions, and SQL
Only the theoretical foundations of distributed computing, without the practical PySpark skills used in real pipelines
Only SQL syntax and the catalog operations, with the DataFrame API saved for the mid-level track instead
Only the NordGrid scenario; transferring the skills learned to other domains requires the senior-level track
questions.correctAnswer: Every operation a junior data engineer uses daily — SparkSession, DataFrames, select/filter, transformations, aggregations, joins, I/O, functions, and SQL
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: the junior track covers the full daily toolkit. Every later module (mid-level, senior) builds on these primitives — performance tuning, streaming, advanced patterns — but the foundation is the same set of ten modules you just completed.
